note_en:
As of today, Windows trusts 322 root certificates issued by 122 different organizations from 47 countries. This number is quite high, and has been steadily growing over the last few years. And it turns out many of those certificates are not needed at all by the vast majority of Windows users, can be distrusted with no ill effects of any sort.
 Each of these CAs is given tremendous power over your Internet traffic, so it makes great sense to minimize the number of CAs your computer trusts. One simple way to achieve this goal is to replace the default Windows list of root CAs with the much stricter Mozilla trust list, which includes 142 roots (52 organizations - 21 countries). An even stricter option is using the Google CTL, which currently includes just 127 root certificates (48 organizations - 21 countries). For the vast majority of users, applying either set is a great way to reduce your exposure to unnecessary CAs, with no negative impact whatsoever.
 Replacing the default Windows list of root CAs with the Mozilla or Google trust lists can be done manually, but is extremely time-consuming and error prone. The free version of RootIQ(*) offers a much simpler way to perform this system change:
 1. Use the Quick select dropdown to select the Mozilla trust set2. Right-click the selection and click Invert selection3. Right-click the selection and click Distrust Click Yes on the confirmation dialog This will distrust all roots that are not part of the Mozilla trust set, except for any Microsoft OS-critical roots.4. Use the Quick select dropdown to select the Mozilla trust set (again)5. Right-click the selection and Trust
  As of this writing, on a standard Windows 10 system, you will end up with 145 trusted roots (rather than 322 roots for the default Microsoft CTL)
 (*) RootIQ, our own root certificate manager for Windows, is now  available. A free version of RootIQ is available for home and evaluation use.
title: 您不需要所有那些根证书
title_en: You don't need all those root certificates

note:
在Slake中你应该使用标点符号。你还应该使用完整的句子和正确的语法。你应该扩展首字母缩略语[1]，在交流情感时，甚至加入一些表情符号以达到效果。你不仅应该在Slake中这样做，而且应该在你所有的专业交流中这样做。
书面交流很难，但在我们工作的数字世界里，它也是至关重要的。可悲的是，像Slake这样的服务所激励的沟通方式很糟糕。
要在对话继续之前发表意见，您可以快速键入，使用缩写，并在拼写错误的情况下单击发送。你一次只想一条信息。你在不同的线程之间跳跃，很容易分心。
这种快而松散的风格有一些明显的缺点。最值得注意的是，它干扰了更有成效的工作，阻碍了深入思考。但一个很大且经常被忽视的负面影响是它对其他人的影响。
当您使用晦涩难懂的缩写或在语法和拼写方面走捷径时，它可能会为您节省几秒钟的时间。问题是，这些小小的节省往往会被你的观众承担的成本所超过。
每个阅读你的信息的人都会花一些有限的时间来解读你说的话。撇开草率的消息可能导致读者错误解释的可能性不谈，更难分析仓促编写的消息-语法不佳、拼写错误、标点符号最少、首字母缩略语晦涩难懂或一次只传递几个单词的消息。
例如，假设您将以下消息发送到包含10个其他人的Slake频道。
并进一步假设“蔡”指的是一项内部计划：反对原始主义的十字军东征。通过全部小写，使用首字母缩写来引用项目，并省略问号，您已经为自己节省了两个单词和几次额外的击键。在最慷慨的假设下，假设这相当于节省了5秒。太多了！
但现在让我们来看看这些快捷方式对您的10人观众的影响。
有两个人认为“蔡”是打字错误，花了几分钟时间试图猜测你打字的实际意思，然后才意识到你指的是内部项目。费用：20秒。
有两个人不知道“蔡”是什么意思。其中一个人花了三分钟在内部维基上追踪，另一个人发了一条私人消息给另一名团队成员询问。费用：4分钟。
有一个人忽略了您的要求，因为他们在扫描频道时没有意识到这是一个问题。成本：无价之宝。
有五个人明白你的意思，但在将你的要求内在化之前，他们仍然必须在心理上扩展缩写。费用：5秒。
您节省的5秒相当于大约4.5分钟的成本分摊给您的观众。当然，这是一个做作的例子，当然也有成本更低的情况，但重点是：你的沟通捷径是昂贵的。
负外部性是影响不选择承担该成本的第三方的成本。
每次你走一条沟通捷径，你就是在给你的受众施加微观外部性。捷径越惊人，外部性就越大；受众越多，外部性倍增的次数就越多，总成本就越大。
如果你正在与一位亲密的朋友交流，并选择使用速记，那么你可能没什么问题。不仅你的朋友可能会习惯它(即外部性会很小)，而且观众也会很少(即总成本会很小)。在这种情况下，您的速记完全有可能比您的朋友节省更多的时间。
然而，当你在一对多的场合(如电子邮件或松弛)进行交流时，尤其是在专业场合，草率的写作会带来真正的后果。它优先考虑你的时间，而不是其他所有要阅读该信息的人的时间，而且可能会曲解你的意图或语气。总体而言，这是对贵公司生产力的净消耗。
所以使用正确的拼写和语法。在点击发送之前写下你的全部想法。展开缩略语。使用标点符号。
一个句号，即使在“做吧”的结尾，也会传达出“我已经说出了我想说的一切”。
[2]草率这个词可能听起来很刺耳，但我是故意选择的。并不是每个人都会说公司的母语(例如英语)，至少可以说，对他们来说，使用正确的拼写或语法可能是一种挑战。这里的争论仅限于那些对这门语言有一定程度的掌握，但在书面交流中粗心大意的人，也就是说，粗心大意的，没有系统的；过于随意的。
note_en:
You should use punctuation in Slack. You should also use complete sentences and proper grammar. You should expand acronyms [1] and — when communicating emotion — even mix in some emoji for effect. You should do that not just in Slack, but in all of your professional communication.
  Written communication is hard, but in the digital world we work in it’s also vital. Sadly, the style of communication that services like Slack incentivize is awful.
 To voice an opinion before the conversation moves on, you type fast and use abbreviations and hit send even with misspellings. You think one message at a time. You bounce between different threads and become easily distracted.
 There are some obvious downsides to this fast and loose style. Most notably, it interrupts more productive work and deters deep thinking. But one large and often overlooked downside is the impact it has on others.
  When you use an obscure initialism or take shortcuts on grammar and spelling, it may save you a couple seconds. The problem is those small savings are often outweighed by the cost born by your audience.
 Every person who reads your message spends some finite period of time interpreting what you’re saying. Setting aside the possibility that a sloppy message might lead the reader to an incorrect interpretation, it’s harder to parse messages written hastily — messages with poor grammar, misspellings, minimal punctuation, obscure acronyms, or delivered a few words at a time.
 As an example, assume you send the following message to a Slack channel with 10 other people in it.
  And further assume that “cai” refers to an internal project:  Crusade  Against  Initialisms. By writing in all lower case, using an initialism to refer to the project, and omitting the question mark, you’ve saved yourself two words and a handful of extra keystrokes. Under the most generous assumptions, say that translates to five seconds in savings. That’s a lot!
 But let’s now examine the impact the shortcuts have on your 10-person audience.
 Two people thought “cai” was a typo and spent several moments trying to guess what you actually meant to type before realizing you were referring to the internal project.  Cost: 20 seconds
  Two people didn’t know what “cai” stood for. One spent three minutes tracking it down on the internal wiki, and the other sent a private message to another team member to ask.  Cost: 4 minutes
  One person ignored your ask because they didn’t realize it was a question when scanning the channel.  Cost: Priceless
  Five people understood what you meant but still had to mentally expand the initialism before internalizing your ask.  Cost: 5 seconds
 Your 5 seconds of savings amounted to roughly 4.5  minutes of cost spread across your audience. Sure, this is a contrived example and sure there are cases where the cost is lower, but the point stands:  your communication shortcuts are costly.
  A negative externality is the cost that affects a third party who did not choose to incur that cost.
 Every time you take a communication shortcut, you are inflicting a micro-externality on your audience. The more egregious the shortcut, the larger the externality; the larger your audience, the more times the externality is multiplied and the larger the overall cost.
  If you’re communicating with a close friend and choose to use shorthand, you’re probably fine. Not only will your friend probably be accustomed to it (i.e. the externality will be small), but the audience is also small (i.e. the total cost will be small). In this case, it’s entirely possible your shorthand saves you more time than it costs your friend.
 However, when you’re communicating in a one-to-many setting such as email or Slack, and particularly in a professional setting, sloppy writing [2] has real consequences. It prioritizes your time above the time of  every other person that will be reading that message and might misinterpret your intent or tone. In aggregate, it is a net drain on productivity at your company.
 So use proper spelling and grammar. Write out your entire thought before hitting send. Expand acronyms. Use punctuation.
 A period, even at the end of “Do it,” communicates, “I’ve said everything I wanted to say.”
   [2] The term "sloppy" might sound harsh, but I chose it deliberately. Not everyone speaks the company language (e.g. English) natively, and for them using proper spelling or grammar might be challenging to say the least. The argument here is scoped to people who have some degree of mastery over the language but are being  sloppy with their written communication, which is to say "careless and unsystematic; excessively casual".
title: 在松弛中使用标点符号

Trusted Contacts是一款适用于Android的个人安全应用程序。它允许您将某些联系人标记为信任，然后这些人可以在紧急情况下请求您的位置。基本功能与谷歌地图位置共享没有太大不同，但当值得信赖的联系人在2016年-Google+黑暗时代-推出时，谷歌地图并没有内置位置共享。(Google Latitude，最初的Google地图位置共享功能，于2009年首次亮相，但在2013年被扼杀，转而支持Google+位置共享。到了2016年，Google+即将退出，位置共享在2017年又回到了谷歌地图。)除了主动的位置共享之外，还增加了一个值得信任的联系人来声明紧急位置请求，其他用户将不得不拒绝，否则它将自动共享。
这款应用程序在Play Store上的评分为3.8分，下载量超过100万次，这还不足以将其从谷歌严酷的收割者手中拯救出来。谷歌向用户发送电子邮件称，由于谷歌地图现在内置了位置共享功能，因此不再需要值得信赖的联系人应用程序。这款应用程序将在43天后关闭，也就是2020年12月1日。
note_en:
Trusted Contacts was  a personal safety app for Android. It let you flag certain contacts as "trusted," and those people could then request your location in an emergency situation. The base functionality was not all that different from Google Maps location sharing, but when Trusted Contacts launched in 2016—the Google+ dark ages—Google Maps did not have location sharing built in. (Google Latitude, the original Google Maps location sharing functionality, debuted in 2009, but that  was killed in 2013 in favor of Google+ location sharing. Google+ was on the way out by 2016, and location sharing returned to  Google Maps in 2017.) The one addition besides a proactive location sharing was for a trusted contact to declare an emergency location request, which the other user would have to deny or it would automatically share.
 The app has a 3.8 rating  on the Play Store and over a million downloads, which is not good enough to save it from the Google grim reaper. Google  sent out emails to users saying that since location sharing was now built into Google Maps, the Trusted Contacts app was no longer need. The app will shut down in just 43 days, on December 1, 2020.
title: 谷歌关闭其紧急位置共享应用Trusted Contacts
title_en: Google shuts down Trusted Contacts, its emergency location sharing app

她说，“我们看到了围绕国家合法化的乐观情绪，感觉到一波娱乐用途合法化将是”看到毒品改革所需的最后一步“。这对多克里来说是个好消息，他正计划成立一家投资大麻、CBD、迷幻剂和性科技等类别的公司。
她在2019年6月宣布了一项2500万美元的基金，但国家政策格局发生了很大变化。
“围绕选举的尖酸刻薄和分歧确实没有留下实质性讨论的空间。我认为这种情况最终会改变，但在选举完成(如果有的话)之前，不要对太多的政策辩论寄予厚望，“她说。“在一个不确定的时期，我们正在后退一小步.”
与许多风投公司一样，副风险投资公司提高了为哪些初创公司提供资金的标准，但几位投资者告诉TechCrunch，他们在总统竞选的最后几天如何做出决定的问题上存在分歧。在经历了一个繁荣的夏天之后，一些人说势头正在增强，而另一些人告诉我们，对初创公司的期望从未像现在这样高。
“如果说有什么不同的话，那就是步伐正在加快，”灵感资本的亚历克萨·冯·托贝尔(Alexa Von Tobel)说。她说，传统上，创始人在临近寒假的时候会缩减募集资金的力度，因为投资者的度假心态正在发挥作用。她说，今年，“我认为，我们将继续看到创始人利用目前充裕的资金流，支撑资源，这样他们就可以在坚实的基础上进入2021年。”
虽然这对创始人来说可能是个好消息，但冯·托贝尔表示，灵感资本在内部并没有对选举给予太多重视。
她说：“我们认为自己是耐心的资本，无论时机如何，我们都专注于寻找最好的公司。”“虽然我们知道选举将制造噪音，并对企业产生长期影响，但目前它在我们的进程中没有一席之地。”
灵感资本在早期环境中投资范围更广，这对其度过危机和动荡的投资能力起到了一定作用。看起来，拥有更多利基投资主题的公司更有可能在选举前改变步伐。
note_en:
“We saw quite a bit more optimism around national legalization, with the feeling that a wave of states legalizing recreational use would be the final push needed” to see drug reform, she said. It was good news for Dockery, who was planning to launch a firm investing in categories like cannabis, CBD, psychedelics and sextech.
 She announced a $25 million fund in June 2019, but the national policy landscape had shifted considerably.
 “The vitriol and division around the election really haven’t left room for substantive discussions. I think this will eventually change, but don’t have high hopes for much policy debate until the election is complete, if at all,” she said. “In a time of uncertainty, we’re taking a small step back.”
 Along with many VC firms, Vice Ventures has raised the bar regarding which startups it will fund, but several investors told TechCrunch they were split about how they’re making decisions in the closing days of the presidential campaign. After  a booming summer, some said momentum is increasing, while others told us that expectations have never been higher for startups.
 “If anything, the pace is increasing,” said Alexa Von Tobel of Inspired Capital. Traditionally, she said founders scale back on fundraising efforts close to the winter holidays because investors’ vacation mentality is kicking in. This year, “ I think we’ll continue to see founders taking advantage of the ample flow of capital right now and shore up resources so they can enter 2021 on strong footing,” she said.
 While that may be good news for founders, Von Tobel said Inspired Capital is not giving too much weight to the election internally.
 “We think of ourselves as patient capital, focused on looking for the best companies no matter the timing,” she said. “While we know the election will create noise and have an impact on businesses long-term, it does not have a place in our process right now.”
 Inspired Capital invests more broadly in the early-stage environment, which plays a part in its ability to invest through crises and turbulence. It seems that firms that have more niche investment theses have been more likely to change their pace ahead of the election.
title: 风投们是否会在2020年大选的最后几天削减支票？
title_en: Are VCs cutting checks in the closing days of the 2020 election?
